intro:
The wrath of God is God's holy, settled, and righteous opposition to sin — His personal reaction of judgment against all evil. Far from a fitful temper or cruelty, divine wrath is the necessary expression of God's holiness and justice: because He is perfectly good, He cannot be indifferent to wickedness. Scripture speaks of this wrath frequently and soberly, for it is the dark backdrop against which the mercy of the gospel shines. To remove God's wrath from the Bible is to empty the cross of its meaning.

In the Old Testament, God's wrath is revealed against sin from the earliest pages — the flood, the destruction of Sodom, and the plagues on Egypt all display His judgment. At Sinai He proclaims Himself "merciful and gracious, slow to anger," yet One who "will by no means clear the guilty" (Exodus 34:6–7). His wrath burns especially against idolatry and covenant-breaking (Deuteronomy 29:24–28), and the Psalms warn, "God is a righteous judge, and a God who feels indignation every day" (Psalm 7:11). Yet His anger is never capricious: "his anger is but for a moment, and his favor is for a lifetime" (Psalm 30:5), and He relents toward the repentant, as at Nineveh.

The prophets gather this judgment into the theme of the coming "day of the LORD," a day of wrath against the proud and the wicked (Zephaniah 1:14–15; Nahum 1:2–3). The New Testament confirms and deepens this. Paul opens his gospel exposition by declaring that "the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men" (Romans 1:18), showing all humanity to be "by nature children of wrath" (Ephesians 2:3). Even Jesus speaks of wrath remaining on the unbelieving (John 3:36), and Revelation portrays its final outpouring (Revelation 6:16–17).

The glory of the gospel is that God Himself provides the way of escape from His own wrath. At the cross Jesus bore the judgment due to sinners, becoming the propitiation — the wrath-bearing, wrath-removing sacrifice — for sin (Romans 3:25; 1 John 4:10). Thus those justified by His blood "shall be saved by him from the wrath of God" (Romans 5:9), for "God has not destined us for wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ" (1 Thessalonians 5:9). Rightly understood, the wrath of God is not opposed to His love but reveals its costliness: the holy God who must judge sin has, in love, given His Son to bear that judgment, so that mercy and justice meet at the cross.
